skill.name: python-development-python-scaffold
skill.description: Scaffold production-ready Python projects with uv, FastAPI, Django, library, CLI, testing, linting, and environment configuration guidance.
skill.summary: Create structured Python project templates with modern tooling and clear development workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical and high-risk patterns, but review found no evidence of malicious intent, exfiltration, or prompt injection. Most alerts come from Markdown scaffolding examples, placeholder .env.example values, and project command snippets. The remaining risk is medium because the skill instructs agents to run shell commands and create or delete project files in the user's workspace.

medium_findings.title: Workspace-Modifying Shell Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to initialize projects, install dependencies, run framework commands, and execute Makefile targets. These commands are legitimate for scaffolding, but they can modify the current workspace and install packages.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain concrete shell and Makefile commands. The commands support the skill purpose, but they perform file, dependency, and cleanup operations.

low_findings.title: Example Environment File Includes Secret Fields
low_findings.description: The skill includes an .env.example template with DATABASE_URL and SECRET_KEY placeholders. These are not real secrets, but users should replace them and avoid committing real environment files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is a placeholder example rather than credential access. It still touches sensitive configuration names that need careful handling in generated projects.

dangerous_patterns.title: Critical Heuristic Combination Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer combined code execution, network, and credential patterns into a critical alert. Review found these are Markdown examples for scaffolding, a localhost CORS example, and placeholder environment values, not data exfiltration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious combination is explained by ordinary project template content. No external collection endpoint, encoded payload, or instruction to read real secrets was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Ruby Backtick Execution Alerts Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings correspond to Markdown fenced blocks and project examples, not hidden Ruby execution. Some examples include normal shell commands that are covered by the medium finding.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The locations are visible Markdown fences and template sections. There is no Ruby code or dynamic backtick execution in the skill file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak cryptography alerts point to description text and generic project metadata. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic implementation was found at the reported lines.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain a skill description or placeholder project description only. There is no cryptographic code or algorithm selection at those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Localhost Network URL Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The hardcoded URL is a localhost development origin in an .env.example CORS setting. It does not send data to an external service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL is explicitly http://localhost:3000 inside an example configuration. This is local development configuration, not network exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment File Access Alerts Mostly Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The .env findings refer to .env.example mentions and a template section. No instruction to read an existing .env file or expose real secrets was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe example files and output format. The only sensitive names are placeholders inside .env.example, with no real secret access.

content.value_statement: Starting a Python project often creates inconsistent layouts, missing tests, and unclear tooling choices. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through structured project scaffolds with modern Python defaults.
actual_capabilities: Selects a scaffold style for FastAPI, Django, library, CLI, or generic Python projects.
Provides directory layouts for web APIs, Django apps, packages, and command-line tools.
Suggests pyproject.toml dependencies and tool configuration for modern Python projects.
Includes pytest, Ruff, README, .gitignore, Makefile, and .env.example guidance.
Shows starter FastAPI and Typer code patterns for common entry points.
limitations: It provides templates and instructions, not a verified project generator.
Dependency versions in examples may need review before production use.
It does not customize security settings for every deployment environment.
Generated shell commands should be reviewed before running in an existing repository.

use_cases.title: Create an API Service
use_cases.description: Generate a FastAPI project layout with routers, schemas, services, settings, tests, and development commands.

use_cases.title: Start a Reusable Package
use_cases.description: Draft a library structure with src layout, package metadata, typed package markers, and test folders.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Starters
use_cases.description: Use consistent pyproject, linting, testing, documentation, and environment examples across new Python repositories.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Project Scaffold
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a simple new project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Scaffold a Python project named inventory-tools using uv, pytest, Ruff, README, and a clean src layout.

prompt_templates.title: FastAPI Service
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for an API backend.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a FastAPI scaffold for an orders API with versioned routes, settings, database module, service layer, and test structure.

prompt_templates.title: Django Application
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a web app.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Django 5 project scaffold for an internal admin app with core app, environment settings, pytest, Ruff, and deployment-ready dependencies.

prompt_templates.title: Team Template Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this proposed Python scaffold against our team standards, then refine dependencies, directories, Makefile tasks, tests, and configuration choices.

output_examples.input: Scaffold a small FastAPI service for user profiles.
output: A versioned API directory layout with routers, endpoints, schemas, models, services, and tests.
A pyproject dependency plan covering FastAPI, Pydantic settings, SQLAlchemy, Alembic, pytest, httpx, and Ruff.
Development instructions for setup, linting, formatting, testing, and local server execution.

output_examples.input: Create a Python library starter package.
output: A src-based package layout with package metadata, typed package marker, tests, README, and license guidance.
A build configuration plan using Hatchling with pytest, Ruff, and mypy as development tools.
A command-line project structure with Typer and Rich dependencies.
A main command entry point plan, script registration guidance, tests, and repeatable development commands.

best_practices: Review generated commands before running them in repositories that already contain work.
Replace all example secrets and database URLs before any deployment.
Pin or review dependency versions according to your team's release policy.
anti_patterns: Do not run cleanup targets without checking the current working directory.
Do not commit real .env files or production credentials.
Do not accept the default scaffold when the project type or deployment model is unclear.

faq.question: Does this skill generate complete files?
faq.answer: It provides project structures, configuration examples, starter code patterns, and setup guidance for common Python project types.

faq.question: Which Python project types are covered?
faq.answer: It covers FastAPI services, Django applications, reusable libraries, CLI tools, and generic Python applications.

faq.question: Does it require uv?
faq.answer: The examples prefer uv for project creation and dependency management, but teams can adapt the structure to other tools.

faq.question: Is it safe for existing repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill suggests commands that create files, initialize git, install dependencies, and remove cache directories.

faq.question: Does it include security configuration?
faq.answer: It includes environment configuration examples, but production security settings must be reviewed for each project.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is suitable for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows that scaffold or review Python projects.
